pages.full_text:
swelled out. French bread. Bugs, Surfs, sunset, sunshine. village, Barn, marina. Wonderful campsite for children, - triple slide into pool, games, climbing frame. Log cabins. Tent cabins.

__Wednesday__
We set off expecting to cycle on a Voit Verte near [?Taijf] Sault - Brenoy, but when we stopped to Viarthona [?Viathona] buy bread at Sault-Breny while I was photographing the bridge, I saw cyclists. So we went on a lovely, gentle bike ride up + down + along the island. On the island there was a specially laid out canoeing course, 2 of them, with rocks + rapids.
On to have lunch near alot of lakes at Dombes Abbey. The monks were invited in 1860, to drain the lakes because the average age was only 20-30. Cistercians. After lunch we went in - they had a prayer room of icons, remembering the 6 monks killed in N Africa. A very plain church. Espaliered apple trees. One monk
pages.notes: Top of page is a continuation from a previous page — no heading for the first paragraph. The place near 'Sault - Brenoy' has an unclear word before it that could read '[?Taijf]' or similar; could be a route/track name. 'Viarthona' or '[?Viathona]' is uncertain — possibly a village name near Sault-Brénod. 'Sault-Breny' appears to be a variant spelling of Sault-Brenoy. No handwritten page number visible on this page (top is cut off). Year cannot be determined from this page alone; context suggests this is a France trip but year is not anchored.

pages.full_text:
was taken off to Belsen for working with the Resistance + got the Croix de Guerre. 2 other monks were killed.

We shopped - for England, pisacio, pate + garlic, + reached St. Jean de Vaux.
photo_captions: Cistercian Abbey Notre Dame des Dombes 01330 le Plantay
pages.notes: Page contains an aerial photograph of the Cistercian Abbey Notre Dame des Dombes, with a handwritten caption written vertically to the left of the photo: 'Cistercian Abbey Notre Dame des Dombes 01330 le Plantay'. The phone/postal code 01330 refers to the commune of Le Plantay in the Ain department, France. 'pisacio' is as written (likely pistachio/pistacchio). 'St. Jean de Vaux' could also read 'St. Jean de Vaux' — place in France (possibly Saint-Jean-de-Vaux in Saône-et-Loire).
